speaker: interviewer
text: So where have-- have we been?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going to tell us about the occupation of your area.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After the Ribbentrop-Molotov Pact--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a few days-- the end-- the 23 or something of August, 1939, in which they divided Poland, which was later a few days invaded by the Germans and then invaded from the other side by the Russians. They divided Poland. They also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did something to the territories of Romania. Romania between the two wars, between the First and the Second World War, called Great Romania, had also-- included also Bukovina and Bessarabia. Bessarabia was Russian-speaking. Bukovina was German-speaking. Bessarabia they took from the Russian and Bukovina from the Austria-Hungarian empire, so these two regions belong to Romania. According to the Ribbentrop-Molotov Pact, this we think so, northern Romania, with its capital Tschernowitz, went over to Russia. And southern Bukovina remained Romania. Our little place, Vatra-Dornei, as well as Arbore and Balaceana, they belonged to southern Romania, so we remained under Romania rule until we were deported. But in 1941, in June, 1941, when the Germans invaded Russia, the Romanians became their allies and they went with them into Russia. So it was somehow a combination of German and-- and Romanian administration, where the Germans determined what the Romanians have to do. After the front went far into Russia, going close to Moscow, the area between the Dniester and the Bug called Transnistria, that means over the Dniester, was given to the Romanian that they should rule it, again under the same conditions that the German had the superior domination. But the Romanian, they were directly involved into that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Marshal Ion Antonescu, I-O-N, Antonescu, A-N-T-O-N-E-S-C-U, who was the-- the leader or the fuhrer of Romania at that time-- he by the way was tried to death as a war criminal after the war. He decided to deport the remaining Jews from northern Bukovina, which have been occupied by the Germans and the Romanian, as well as the south-- the Jews from southern Bukovina, as a first step to the further deportation of all the Jews of Romania into Transnistria, into these occupied territories of Transnistria. The northern-- the Jews of Bessarabia and of northern Bukovina except for Tschernowitz, which was the capital, where most of them were just exterminated-- personally, my father's sister Miryam and her husband and her two children, Rachel and Yakov-- that I loved to play with, they used to come to my grandfather-- they were probably drowned or shot into Dniester.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any case, but the thousand part, they were all communities who continue to live under the-- the war conditions in the-- in our places. And they deported place by place into that Transnistria, including the Jews who remained somehow alive in somehow in Bessarabia and northern Bukovina. Tschernowitz had to come the last. Then they started in Moldova with Dorohoi, D-O-R-O-H-O-I, and some little places around Dorohoi, like [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and they also were deported into Transnistria. But then something happened and we do not know exactly what. Some claim that he got some warning from the United States of America, this Antonescu, that he would have to-- to pay for what he was doing and the deportation stopped. So only a part of Tschernowitz has been deported. But the other part, those who succeeded to remain, they remained in Tschernowitz and [NON-ENGLISH], which is the place of my wife, was not anymore-- which is close next to Dorohoi, was not deported anymore. And from this point, no other Jews were deported from Romania into Transnistria. So this explains you a little bit the picture of how this worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Vatra-Dornei. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. A day before Hoshana Rabbah, we were all advised that we have to prepare our things. We have to leave. We will be all evacuated. That was the term.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave this order?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanian authorities. It was also published by the drummer of the city. There was a drummer who used to-- still we-- I see this drummer. And he announced this to everybody, that we have to hand over to the-- to the city hall all our foreign currency we have, all silver, all gold, those precious metals, and if not, it is by death punishment, and that we have to be 24 hours later at the big station-- there were two train stations in that place-- at the big station for to leave the place. Everybody had the right to take with him a parcel of five kilograms. That's about 10, 12 pounds or something like this. I remember that this very-- this morning, that was the last time that we made our prayer in our temple. I led the prayer. And I did it in such a speed that I don't think that it would ever be able to do that-- this in such a speed. We all profaned Shabbat. We had no choice. And we prepared what we have to prepare, because we had to go to the train station on Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll come to that. And my mother made up such little bags for everybody. Every child had to carry its own bag. And naturally, I had also my bag. And suddenly came in the-- Dr. Druchman was his name, the gabbai, or the president, of our synagogue with a little Sefer Torah. This Sefer Torah has been written about 150 years ago counting from today at the inauguration of this temple by one of his ancestors, who was the founder and the first gabbai of this temple. And he said to my-- to my father in Yiddish, Herr Leibisch, I would like to save at least once Sefer Torah from our synagogue. I don't know what will happen. I know that if you will take it, it will be saved. So my father turned to me. He said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]-- he used to use this diminutive-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This will be your bag.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took all the things that my mother gave me, took them out from the bag and put in this little Sefer Torah, And with that, we went to the concentration-- to the-- may I just-- I-- we saw-- with that, we were deported. So I was not in a concentration camp, but in a ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We left the-- the train left the city the evening of Hoshana Rabbah or the-- and in that train, there were around 1,000, 900 Jewish people. We were in wagons for cattle, pressed together, youngsters, older people, sick people, healthy people, you name it. And my father became immediately quite the commander of this wagon. Every wagon had an elder. The Romanian army, they were responsible for this train. We had the right to open up a little place to have some air. This they let us. And I remember in the morning, we came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at a train station, a central train station called Darmanesti, D-A-R-M-A-N-E-S-T-I. And suddenly, the train stopped in the field. And my father says to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. Look. There is a tree with hoshanot. And I jumped out of the train and broke up a branch of it. And I run back and the train started to move. My father caught me up back to the-- to the wagon. And so under these conditions, we had the Sefer Torah and we had a hoshanot. You cannot imagine. My father had one prayer book. Not everybody had prayer books. People wanted to just to kiss the Torah. It was something unbelievable. And so the train went through Bukovina into Bessarabia and brought us to a place called Atachi, A, double T, A-C-H-I, which is at the border of the Dniester. There was a terrible mud because of the rains and because the Dniester flooded up. There was a flood. And that year, there was a lot of rains in that year. And you don't know what the [NON-ENGLISH] mud is. You just get drowned there. And there were thousands of people, coming from all places, some of them led by Israel-- by-- sorry-- by Romanian army soldiers, some of them just running to find their families, some of them didn't have any objective, but just being there. And some of them were beaten up. And some of them were yelled at and cursed at and you name it. And in this mess comes in a new train with about to close to 2,000 people. And we were looking for someplace where we could be. And my father said, let's be in this corner. We are going to look for some house. Maybe we find some empty house, because the entire place was empty, ruined by war, by the flood, et cetera. And as we walk in the streets of this Atachi-- it's called in Jewish literature, Yiddish literature, it's called [NON-ENGLISH]. As we walk, we saw that on many houses, destroyed houses, on many walls there are inscriptions, different things, written by fingers which were dipped into blood. And I remember one of those inscriptions [NON-ENGLISH]. I, Moshe Israel, Ben-- he said also the father, I have it written somewhere-- and my wife Fiege and my children, vengeance-- venge us the murderers come. Imagine what-- what kind of a situation. We come from our houses, which still where regular houses, regular daily life. We come into that mess of people, of events, we didn't know where we were living, and here these messages on the walls telling us what happened in this place when the Romanian and the German exterminated all these Jews. And they called us for vengeance, that we should take [INAUDIBLE] because of their deaths. We were shocked to death. And my father stood and said kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And Jews stood around. I don't know who they were. But suddenly my father said to me like this, [NON-ENGLISH] We say kaddish, we say [NON-ENGLISH]. Know But on [NON-ENGLISH], which is the day afterwards, we dance also with the Torah. And we went back to the place where we had this little Torah. From nowhere we got some table. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we danced with the Torah in this scenery, in this-- on this background, in these chaos. It was like a struggle to maintain human dignity. It was a struggle to maintain the relation with our past. It was a struggle to be-- to remain just human beings. And I don't know where from my father took this strength. I just know now-- later is so many years, I can appreciate what he did then to us. It was a struggle for survival, for moral survival, let-- let alone the physical survival. The day after was [NON-ENGLISH] and then was [NON-ENGLISH]. My father did all he could and he succeeded that we shouldn't be moved over Dniester Dmn during the holidays. So we stayed in this place until after [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were you all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all dispersed. There were hundreds and thousands of people around. But I-- there was no counting in that time. Everybody was for himself to keep this little family together that we shouldn't lose each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some food we brought with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the sanitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there any degree lower than zero? You have it. Sanitation, eh, that's-- we were still relatively well-clothed. We were still relatively clean. We were still could change your shirt. But forget about it. That's-- that wasn't the problem. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we prayed around the Sefer Torah all the prayers of the holidays. [NON-ENGLISH]. And I remember my father was reading from the Torah. And he was also Hattan Torah. And to see him again these-- all these people around. There was no institution, no nothing, no community, no link, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And being around the Sefer Torah and having holidays-- years later-- Who got the [NON-ENGLISH]? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Years later in my synagogue in Israel, I got to be [NON-ENGLISH]. This Sefer Torah is actually in my synagogue in Israel. It's a long story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should read it in that Sefer Torah. And I went. I made a [NON-ENGLISH]. Have As I just start to read [NON-ENGLISH], I saw my father there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I couldn't utter a single word. The community didn't know anything about that. I'm going to stop the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started to read, as I said. And it was just impossible for me to read. I started to cry. And every word was just as difficult as to cross the-- the Red Sea. Nobody understood what was going on. Everybody thought that I am so moved by the fact that they gave me the honor of being Hattan Torah and therefore I was crying. And slowly, slowly I finished reading. I had no problems with [NON-ENGLISH]. And then my wife and me, we gave a big kiddush in the synagogue. And that is as usual, the Hattan Torah has to say [NON-ENGLISH]. And naturally, I prepared something. But then I said to the entire congregation, my dear friends, this time I won't say any [NON-ENGLISH]. But I'm going to tell you the story about the Torah. And for the first time I told the story to my congregation. Until nowadays, it's a matter of about 20 years ago. They still remember the story with that Torah. And now when I participated at the inauguration of the-- dedication of the Sefer Torah in Mamaroneck, State of New York, so I told again the story of that Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next, I think it was about a day after Simchat Torah, we were brought by the Romanian soldiers to the Dniester, put into little boats. I think they were made of gummy, of rubber, but I'm not sure. We were afraid that we would be just-- we heard stories about people not reaching the other side of the shore. But we came on the other side of the shore to a place that we didn't even know about its existence before called Mogilev-Podol'skii. There all-- a lot of people came from all sides. They were brought there. And the policy of the Romanian authorities at that time was to continue to chase the Jews in the other places of that big-- vast area called Transnistria. They were chased to [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], all kind of names that I forgot already. And each group, people tried to get together. They bribed a lot of Romanian soldiers so they should go together either with their families or friends. And so there were little ghettos in all kinds of places in Transnistria. My father wanted to remain in Mogilev. Our idea was always the closer we are still back home, the more chances we have to get back home. And then it was also because the-- the-- there was many people there. And we wanted to be in a big Jewish community. We felt somehow more secure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a choice about where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But it was, as we say it, [NON-ENGLISH]. You should know how to-- to manage the situation. Exactly how it happened, I don't know. But immediately we had to face-- after a very short period, we have to face a number of very severe survival problems. One was simply food. Where do we take food?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it was the winter coat, clothing. Where to live? And that-- there are certain periods that I really don't remember even well. I just remember living in many places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some place we lived there were about 20, 30, or 40 families in a big hall and we separated with-- with some blankets each little apartment. You call that apartment, whatsoever. It was very difficult to live and we have no privacy whatsoever, but you were-- you witnessed everything which happens in life of so many families. Finally, and this is what I remember best of all, we succeeded in having a little room in a big house called the Casino. In this house in Mogliev, there were many Jewish families living there, only Jewish families, of the deported Jews of the ghetto. The ghetto started to organize itself. There was a committee, a Jewish committee, who was appointed by the Romanian. And there were no democratic elections for that, but somehow we tried to organize our life to make it-- decent is too much, but to make it livable, that you could just live in a more-- more or less organized way. I remember that I got to my bar mitzvah, which was about a year and a half, a year before we were deported, year and a half or something like this. I got a watch, a golden watch from my parents. There was these watches that had a-- a chain that you put it in your little pocket. And it had a cover of gold. And I liked it very much, naturally. I sold this watch for two big breads, breads like this. And from these breads we ate quite a good number of days. We-- we [INAUDIBLE] it and we divided it into little rations for each day for each child. And I remember it was a Friday night and one or two of my sisters started to cry because they were hungry. So my parents took pieces from their ration and gave it to the children. That was the way we started to organize ourselves. Now, the other problem was the winter. That winter we had in that place temperatures going below zero centigrades, about 32, 34 centigrades. That's a lot-- I have no idea what that means in Fahrenheit, but very, very cold, to the extent that when you touched with your bare hand some iron door handle, the hand leaked to that just because of the cold. Many, many people just didn't survive. They froze outside. If they didn't have where to stay or if they didn't have what-- what to put on themselves, they just didn't wake up in the morning, especially if they drank a little bit of alcohol from somewhere. That was killing-- there were by hundreds and thousands of people who perished this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a long story.
